md: In *Calculus Made Easy*, Silvanus Thompson devotes a whole chapter to the simplest, sneakiest idea in calculus:
> *"If a thing is going to change, then it must change with respect to something. Usually that something is time."*
We already met one rate of change: **speed = distance per little bit of time**, $\dfrac{ds}{dt} = v$. That was the speedometer lesson.

This lesson is about doing the **same trick a second time**. If you differentiate position once, you get velocity. If you differentiate velocity once *more*, you get something brand new — **acceleration**.

blocks.title: The whole lesson in one sentence
blocks.body: Differentiate $s(t)$ once → velocity $v(t)$. Differentiate $v(t)$ once → acceleration $a(t)$. The *same operation* — "a little bit of, per little bit of time" — used **twice in a row**.

md: ## 1. Toss a ball straight up
Throw a ball upward at $25\text{ m/s}$ from a window $20\text{ m}$ above the ground. Physics says its height is
(The $-4.9\,t^2$ is Earth's gravity: $\tfrac{1}{2}g$ with $g = 9.8\text{ m/s}^2$.)
Now apply the power rule one term at a time:
$\dfrac{ds}{dt} = 25 - 9.8\,t \ = \ v(t)$ — **the velocity at time $t$**.
At $t = 0$: $v = 25$ (the throw). At $t \approx 2.55\text{ s}$: $v = 0$ (top of the arc — the ball is momentarily not moving). After that, $v$ goes negative — the ball is falling.
**Differentiate again:**
$\dfrac{dv}{dt} = -9.8 \ = \ a(t)$ — **the acceleration**.
It's a constant: $-9.8\text{ m/s}^2$. Gravity isn't picky about whether the ball is going up or down — it tugs at the same rate the entire flight.

blocks.title: Read the three plots together
blocks.body:
**Top — height $s(t)$:** parabola. Up, peak, down.

**Middle — velocity $v(t)$:** straight line, sloping down. Positive while rising, **zero at the peak**, negative on the way down.

**Bottom — acceleration $a(t)$:** flat line at $-9.8$. The same constant the whole flight.

Move the time slider. Watch the cursor cross the three plots together. **At the peak of the height curve, the velocity is zero but the acceleration is NOT zero** — gravity is still pulling. That's why the ball doesn't hover.

md: ## 2. The notation grows by one tick each time
There are three ways mathematicians write "differentiated twice with respect to $t$":
$a(t) = \dfrac{d^2 s}{dt^2} = \ddot{s} = s''(t)$
$\dfrac{d^2 s}{dt^2}$ — "a little bit of (a little bit of $s$), per (little bit of $t$) squared". Built straight out of the symbols.
$\ddot{s}$ — two dots over $s$. Newton's notation. Used mostly in physics.
$s''(t)$ — two primes. Used everywhere in pure math.
All three mean **rate of change of the rate of change**.

blocks.title: Stacking primes
blocks.body:
$s$ — position

$s'$ — velocity

$s''$ — acceleration

$s'''$ — *jerk* (rate of change of acceleration). When an elevator starts smoothly, designers worry about jerk: $s''$ rising slowly feels good; $s''$ jumping suddenly feels like a kick.

$s''''$ — sometimes called *snap*. Yes really. Mostly used in fancy vibration analysis.

md: ## 3. Three motions, three personalities
Switch the **motion** dropdown in the widget. Three profiles, three different *shapes* of $s, v, a$.
### A. Ball thrown up (already done)
Parabolic $s$, linear $v$, **constant** $a$. The thing producing the motion is a *constant force* (gravity).
### B. Drag race — constant acceleration from rest
$s(t) = t^2$, so $v = 2t$, $a = 2$. Same family as the thrown ball, except $a > 0$ — you're speeding up. **Each differentiation strips one power of $t$:** $t^2 \to 2t \to 2 \to 0$.
### C. Spring oscillation — the killer example
$s(t) = 2\sin t$. Apply the rule for differentiating $\sin$:
$v(t) = \dfrac{ds}{dt} = 2\cos t$
$a(t) = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = -2\sin t = -s(t)$
Look at that. **Acceleration is the negative of position.** Switch to "oscillation" in the widget — the red and blue curves are mirror images across the $t$-axis.

blocks.title: $\boxed{\;a = -\omega^2 s\;}$ — the law of every oscillator
blocks.body: Springs, pendulums, AC current, sound waves, light waves, the strings of a guitar — *anything* that oscillates obeys $a = -\omega^2 s$, with $\omega$ called the angular frequency. The further from equilibrium ($s = 0$), the harder it's pulled back. We discovered this just by differentiating $\sin$ twice. No physics required.

md: ## 4. Why a moving object "feels" acceleration, not velocity
When a plane is cruising at $250\text{ m/s}$, you don't feel a thing — drinks sit still, you can walk down the aisle. Velocity itself is invisible to your body.

But the moment the plane accelerates for takeoff, you're shoved back into your seat. **Acceleration is what bodies feel** — because Newton's $F = m\,a$ is the force on you, and force is what you notice.

So when you sit in a car, your stomach is a tiny acceleration-meter: it reports $\ddot{s}$, not $\dot{s}$. Calculus's second derivative is the only one with a physical name ("g-force") that everyone uses, even people who hate math.

blocks.title: How to read a position graph in one glance
blocks.body:
Don't squint at numbers — just look at the SHAPE.

**Straight line, going up** → constant positive velocity, zero acceleration. "Cruise."

**Curve bending up (concave up)** → velocity is increasing → positive acceleration. "Speeding up."

**Curve bending down (concave down)** → velocity is decreasing → negative acceleration. "Braking" or "falling back."

The second derivative is exactly what concavity *means*. Open up the widget and watch the height curve: the moment it stops bending up and starts bending down, that's the moment acceleration goes from positive to negative. (For a tossed ball, gravity is always negative — so the height curve bends down the whole time.)

md: ## 5. The pattern
Anywhere a quantity changes, **its rate of change is a quantity too** — and *that* one has its own rate of change. You can keep going.
| symbol | what it measures | felt how? |
| $s(t)$ | position | seen |
| $s'(t)$ | velocity | speedometer |
| $s''(t)$ | acceleration | shove in the back |
| $s'''(t)$ | jerk | sudden "kick" — bad ride |

Calculus didn't invent these — you've always known them. It just gave them one shared name (*the derivative*) and one shared way to compute them (*differentiate the previous one*). Same trick, repeated.

**That is Thompson's whole point in Chapter 8.** If you can differentiate once, you can differentiate twice. Calculus does not get harder when you do it again — it just gives you new physical quantities to talk about.
